details.body: Four "resume from failed run" tests in `tests/unit/workflow_engine_test.rs` are broken by the retry limit increase. The tests `resume_from_failed_run_skips_completed_stages` (line 3027), `resume_from_failed_docs_change_run_skips_completed_stages` (line 1033), `resume_from_failed_ci_improvement_run_skips_completed_stages` (line 1101), and `resume_from_failed_quick_dev_run_skips_completed_stages` (line 1407) all use `with_transient_failure(stage, 3)` but `TransportFailure` max_attempts is now 5. The 4th attempt succeeds, so `assert!(first_result.is_err())` fails. Fix: bump the transient failure count from 3 to 5 in each of these tests so the run exhausts retries and fails as expected.

The same four broken "resume from failed run" tests also lack `start_paused = true` on their `#[tokio::test]` attribute. With the new exponential backoff, each test incurs ~35 seconds of real-time delay (5s + 10s + 20s between retries). All four tests need `#[tokio::test(start_paused = true)]` to avoid real wall-clock backoff delays in CI.

src/adapters/process_backend.rs:486-499 and 1121-1132 still report a missing CLI as AppError::BackendUnavailable, and src/contexts/agent_execution/service.rs:166-169 plus 362-372 preserve/remap that to TransportFailure; src/shared/error.rs:300-305 then keeps BackendUnavailable retryable. In practice, a normal missing `claude`/`codex` binary still burns all 5 transport retries, so the new BinaryNotFound rule only applies to rarer spawn/exit-code paths (src/adapters/process_backend.rs:935-944, 1381-1384) and the fast-fail acceptance criterion is not actually met. Carry BinaryNotFound through the availability/build-command path as well.

src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:4619-4639 adds a plain tokio::time::sleep(backoff) before retrying, but that sleep never watches the cancellation token. If cancellation arrives after will_retry is computed (for example right after stage_failed is logged), the run now sits idle for 5-60 seconds before the next loop notices cancellation. Make the backoff wait cancellable with tokio::select! or an equivalent re-check.

Timeouts currently take the generic cleanup path instead of the failure-preserving one. The new timeout branch in src/adapters/process_backend.rs:987-1001 returns an error directly from spawn_and_wait(), and invoke() handles every such error with prepared.cleanup() at src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1150-1163. For Codex/OpenRouter, cleanup() deletes the schema/last-message temp files (src/adapters/process_backend.rs:55-63) rather than moving them into runtime/failed like cleanup_failed_invocation() does (src/adapters/process_backend.rs:66-90). A hung backend therefore becomes less inspectable than an ordinary non-zero exit. Route timeout failures through a preserving cleanup path.

BUG: Cancellation during retry backoff reports wrong failure class in run_failed journal event. In engine.rs:4642-4655, when cancellation is detected during the backoff sleep, `fail_run_result(&error, ...)` is called with the original backend error (e.g. TransportFailure). This causes `failure_label()` to return "transport_failure" instead of "cancellation". The pre-existing test `cancellation_between_retry_attempts_does_not_start_next_attempt` (workflow_engine_test.rs:3870) asserts `run_failed.details["failure_class"] == "cancellation"` and will fail.

The sequence: (1) first attempt returns TransportFailure, (2) `will_retry` computed as true (token not cancelled yet), (3) CancelBetweenRetryAttemptsLogWriter triggers cancellation during stage_failed log write, (4) backoff select! detects cancellation, (5) fail_run_result called with original TransportFailure error → writes "transport_failure" not "cancellation".

In the old code (without the select!/cancellation check), the retry loop continued to the next attempt, where the stub adapter detected cancellation and returned InvocationCancelled, correctly producing "cancellation".

Fix: When cancellation is detected during backoff (engine.rs:4642), construct an AppError::InvocationCancelled and pass that to fail_run_result instead of the original `&error`.

`src/shared/error.rs:299-305` now maps every `AppError::BackendUnavailable` to `FailureClass::BinaryNotFound`. That variant is reused for non-binary outages and config/auth errors, for example `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1164-1172` (missing `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`), `src/adapters/openrouter_backend.rs:326-333` (401/403), and `src/adapters/github.rs:613-624` (GitHub HTTP/JSON failures). After this change those unrelated failures are all mislabeled as `binary_not_found` and become terminal/non-retryable. Restrict `BinaryNotFound` to the actual spawn/exit-127 paths and keep other `BackendUnavailable` cases in their existing class.

`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:4620-4654` regresses cancellation handling during the new retry backoff. If `cancellation_token` fires inside the `tokio::select!`, the code falls through to `fail_run_result(&error, ...)`, so the run is recorded as the prior backend failure instead of `cancellation`. `cargo test --features test-stub --test unit workflow_engine_test::cancellation_between_retry_attempts_does_not_start_next_attempt` now fails with `transport_failure` vs `cancellation`. Return a cancellation error from this branch (or plumb cancellation into `fail_run_result`) before recording the terminal run failure.

`src/adapters/process_backend.rs:352-369`, `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1021-1025`, and `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1434-1437` only signal the direct child PID on timeout/cancel. Any grandchildren spawned by `claude`/`codex` survive as orphaned processes after the parent is SIGKILLed. The tmux adapter already targets the process group in `src/adapters/tmux.rs:990-1002`; process-backend needs equivalent process-group setup/teardown before we can say timed-out backend invocations are actually killed.

`tests/unit/workflow_engine_test.rs:1089-1092`, `tests/unit/workflow_engine_test.rs:1157-1160`, `tests/unit/workflow_engine_test.rs:1468-1470`, and `tests/unit/workflow_engine_test.rs:3084-3086` still assert 4 stage entries after changing the stub failure count from 3 to 5. With the higher retry budget the failed run now enters the stage 5 times and the resumed run adds a 6th entry, so these tests fail under `cargo test --features test-stub --test unit workflow_engine_test::resume_from_failed_*`.

The inner timeout in `spawn_and_wait()` (`process_backend.rs:1018`) and the outer timeout in `service.rs:204` both use `request.timeout` for the same duration, but the outer timeout starts first (before `build_command` and process spawn overhead). This means the service-layer timeout always fires before or simultaneously with the adapter-layer timeout, making the `preserve_failed_artifacts` call on the Timeout error path in `invoke()` (process_backend.rs:1193-1196) unreachable in production. On timeout, Codex schema/message temp files are left in their temp location instead of being preserved to `runtime/failed`, losing diagnostic artifacts.

Fix: Either (a) add a small buffer to the service-level timeout (e.g., `request.timeout + Duration::from_secs(30)`) so the inner timeout fires first and handles cleanup, or (b) move artifact preservation into the `cancel()` method or a dedicated timeout cleanup hook called by the service after timeout detection.

The test `spawn_and_wait_timeout_returns_timeout_failure` (process_backend_test.rs:1990) verifies that a timeout returns `FailureClass::Timeout` but does not assert that failed artifacts are actually preserved to `runtime/failed`. Add assertions checking that the `.failed.raw` file (and for Codex backends, the `.schema.json` / `.last-message.json` files) exist in the project's `runtime/failed` directory after a timeout failure.

src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1018-1023 and src/adapters/process_backend.rs:391-405 / 1434-1437: the new timeout path does not actually kill timed-out children on non-Unix platforms. `spawn_background_reap()` always calls `send_sigkill()`, but `send_sigkill()` returns `Unsupported` under `#[cfg(not(unix))]` and that error is ignored. The spawned task then sits in `child.wait()` forever while still owning the `ManagedChild`, so `kill_on_drop(true)` never fires either. On Windows a backend timeout will therefore return `FailureClass::Timeout` while the `claude`/`codex` process keeps running in the background, which violates the new hard-timeout guarantee and can leak orphaned backend processes.

src/adapters/process_backend.rs:698-709, src/adapters/process_backend.rs:966-975, src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1248-1288, and src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1423-1430: `BinaryNotFound` is now inferred from exit status 127, but actual missing executables are already detected earlier by `resolve_binary()` and by `command.spawn()` returning `ErrorKind::NotFound`. Once the backend process has started, an exit code of 127 is just application-defined output, not proof that the backend binary was missing. Mapping every 127 to non-retryable `BinaryNotFound` means a wrapper script or CLI that exits 127 for its own error path will now fail the run immediately instead of using the new retry budget.

details.message: backend invocation failed for contract 'plan_and_implement' via 'claude': schema validation failed for stage 'plan_and_implement': missing field `change_summary`

details.body: Add a test in `tests/unit/process_backend_test.rs` that verifies when the backend binary truly does not exist (i.e., is not on PATH), `invoke()` returns `FailureClass::BinaryNotFound`. Currently the exit-code-127 test covers post-spawn classification, and retry_policy tests verify BinaryNotFound is terminal, but the actual spawn-failure → BinaryNotFound path in `process_backend.rs:986-987` (`ErrorKind::NotFound` branch) has no direct test. This is the critical non-retryable path that prevents wasting 5 retry attempts on a missing binary.

Map adapter-enforced process timeouts back to `AppError::InvocationTimeout` before they leave the service. `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1037-1051` now returns `InvocationFailed { failure_class: Timeout }`, and `src/contexts/agent_execution/service.rs:211-213` plus `src/contexts/agent_execution/service.rs:369-373` pass that through unchanged. Existing consumers still branch on the timeout variant (for example `src/adapters/worktree.rs:892-900`), so a real CLI hang will now be treated as a generic invocation failure instead of following timeout-specific recovery/classification paths.

Make fatal availability/configuration failures terminal instead of retryable. The service always runs `check_availability()` first (`src/contexts/agent_execution/service.rs:166-169`); `ProcessBackendAdapter::check_availability()` reports missing binaries and missing `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` as `BackendUnavailable` (`src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1171-1193`); and `AppError::failure_class()` still maps `BackendUnavailable` to retryable `TransportFailure` (`src/shared/error.rs:300-305`). After the retry-budget increase in `src/contexts/workflow_composition/retry_policy.rs:45-57`, those fatal setup problems will now burn five attempts plus backoff instead of failing immediately.

In `map_invoke_error` (service.rs:374-381), the promotion from `InvocationFailed{Timeout, details}` to `InvocationTimeout{backend, contract_id, timeout_ms}` silently discards the `details` string. This loses diagnostic context like "claude exceeded timeout of 600s" that was captured at the adapter level. Downstream log entries and error messages will only show the generic "backend invocation timed out" format. Consider either (a) preserving details in a new field on `InvocationTimeout`, or (b) logging the original details before promoting, so operators can still diagnose which binary timed out and see the adapter-level context.

`src/shared/error.rs:300-308` now maps every `AppError::BackendUnavailable` to `FailureClass::BinaryNotFound`, but `BackendUnavailable` is used for transient availability failures too. For example, `src/adapters/openrouter_backend.rs:176-204` returns `BackendUnavailable` for HTTP client construction failures, probe request errors, and any non-2xx `/models` response, and `src/adapters/openrouter_backend.rs:369-380` is called on every `AgentExecutionService::invoke()` via `src/contexts/agent_execution/service.rs:166-169`. With this blanket remap, a transient OpenRouter 429/5xx or network hiccup is now classified as terminal `binary_not_found`, so the workflow never uses the new retry budget. The new non-retryable class needs to be assigned only at actual missing-binary/misconfiguration call sites, not by reclassifying the entire `BackendUnavailable` variant.

In `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1200`, a missing `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` is classified as `FailureClass::BinaryNotFound`. This is semantically incorrect — a missing API key is not a missing binary. Consider introducing a dedicated `FailureClass` variant (e.g., `ConfigurationError`) or at minimum rename the usage site comment to clarify why `BinaryNotFound` is being repurposed as a general 'fatal infrastructure' signal. As-is, log messages and operator diagnostics will report 'binary_not_found' for a missing env var, which is confusing.

The `classify_exit_failure` function at `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1459` still returns `TransportFailure` unconditionally for all exit codes. The project prompt specifically called for differentiating exit 127 (binary not found via shell) and signal-killed processes. The comment above the function explains why exit 127 post-spawn shouldn't be `BinaryNotFound`, which is a reasonable design decision, but the function still takes `_status` (unused parameter) suggesting it was intended to be filled in. At minimum, consider removing the unused parameter or documenting this as an intentional no-op to avoid future confusion.

The `backoff_for_attempt` function at `src/contexts/workflow_composition/retry_policy.rs:110` computes `2u64.saturating_pow(attempt.saturating_sub(1))` then calls `saturating_mul(multiplier.try_into().unwrap_or(u32::MAX))`. The `try_into()` converts `u64` to `u32` — for attempt >= 33, `2^32` overflows `u32` and falls back to `u32::MAX`, which then gets multiplied with the base duration using `u32::MAX` as the multiplier. While the `.min(self.backoff_cap)` at the end caps the result, the intermediate `saturating_mul` with `u32::MAX` is wasteful and the `try_into().unwrap_or(u32::MAX)` pattern is fragile. Since `Duration::saturating_mul` takes `u32`, just compute the multiplier directly as `u32` with `2u32.saturating_pow(...)` to avoid the lossy conversion.

`AgentExecutionService` no longer enforces the caller’s configured timeout for adapters that depend on the service-level guard. In `src/contexts/agent_execution/service.rs:196-221`, the timeout was changed from `request.timeout` to `request.timeout + 30s`, but adapters like the stub backend still only sleep/cancel cooperatively and do not apply their own hard deadline. That makes a 10ms request succeed after ~100ms instead of timing out; `tests/unit/agent_execution_test.rs:292-316` now fails under `cargo test --features test-stub --test unit agent_execution_test::service_times_out_and_requests_backend_cancellation`. This is a real regression in the timeout contract, not just a test issue.

Exit-code classification still does not implement the requested fatal-path handling. `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1452-1460` defines `classify_exit_failure`, but it ignores the `ExitStatus` and always returns `FailureClass::TransportFailure`; the call sites at `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1282-1294` and `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1304-1316` therefore retry exit 127 exactly like a transient transport failure. The new test at `tests/unit/process_backend_test.rs:2038-2068` even codifies this behavior, which contradicts the acceptance criterion that a missing binary / exit 127 should fail immediately without consuming the retry budget.

The new `BinaryNotFound` classification is dropped on some required availability-check paths, so those failures still retry as `TransportFailure`. In `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:5954-5962` and `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:6430-6446`, required prompt-review refiner / final-review planner / arbiter availability errors are rewrapped with `failure_class: None`. Because `AppError::failure_class()` defaults `BackendUnavailable { failure_class: None, .. }` to `TransportFailure` (`src/shared/error.rs:308-313`), a missing binary or missing API key from `check_availability()` becomes retryable again in those stages.

details.message: backend invocation timed out for contract 'plan_and_implement' via 'claude' after 3600000 ms

details.body: src/adapters/tmux.rs:452-453 advertises `enforces_timeout() == true`, but `TmuxAdapter::invoke()` never applies `request.timeout`; it just waits in `wait_for_session_exit()` until the service cancels it (`src/adapters/tmux.rs:518-520`, `src/adapters/tmux.rs:342-379`). Because `AgentExecutionService` adds a 30-second buffer whenever that flag is true (`src/contexts/agent_execution/service.rs:204-223`), tmux runs now exceed the configured timeout by about 30 seconds before cancellation. This regresses the hard-timeout behavior for the `process`/`openrouter` selector in tmux mode (`src/composition/agent_execution_builder.rs:95-108`).

src/adapters/openrouter_backend.rs:64-74 still reports a missing `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` as `BackendUnavailable { failure_class: None }`. `AppError::failure_class()` maps that to `TransportFailure` (`src/shared/error.rs:309-313`), and `RetryPolicy` now retries transport failures 5 times (`src/contexts/workflow_composition/retry_policy.rs:45-57`). In non-tmux `openrouter` mode (`src/composition/agent_execution_builder.rs:114`), a missing API key will therefore still burn the full retry budget instead of failing immediately as a terminal prerequisite error.

details.message: backend invocation timed out for contract 'final_review:reviewer' via 'claude' after 3600000 ms

details.body: The `backoff_for_attempt` function uses `2u32.saturating_pow(attempt.saturating_sub(1))` as the multiplier, then `self.backoff_base.saturating_mul(multiplier)`. When `attempt` is 0 (which shouldn't happen but isn't prevented by the type system), `saturating_sub(1)` wraps to `u32::MAX`, making `saturating_pow` return `u32::MAX`, and the resulting delay saturates to a massive duration before being capped. The cap at 60s saves this, but an `attempt == 0` would produce an unexpectedly large delay (60s) instead of 0s. Consider adding a guard: if `attempt == 0`, return `Duration::ZERO`.

src/adapters/tmux.rs:564-585 still turns every non-zero backend exit into `FailureClass::TransportFailure`. That means `execution.mode = "tmux"` does not get the new exit-127 handling that was added in src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1460-1469, so a fatal "command not found" failure is still retried in tmux mode. The acceptance criteria call out exit 127 as terminal, so the tmux adapter needs the same classification logic instead of hard-coding `TransportFailure`.

src/contexts/agent_execution/service.rs:223-228 now makes the service timeout the canonical timeout for tmux, but src/adapters/tmux.rs:519-598 only runs `prepared.cleanup()` / `prepared.finish()` after `wait_for_session_exit()` returns. When the service timeout fires, that future is aborted before those lines run, and src/adapters/tmux.rs:601-658 only cleans up the tmux session itself. As a result, Codex/OpenRouter tmux timeouts leak the `PreparedCommand` temp files described in src/adapters/process_backend.rs:55-90 and skip failed-artifact preservation entirely.

The backoff `backoff_for_attempt(cursor.attempt)` is called with the *current* attempt number (1-indexed, starting at 1) before `cursor.retry()` increments it. This means the very first retry (after attempt 1 fails) waits 5 seconds. For the common case of a transient JSON parse error that would succeed immediately on retry, this adds unnecessary latency. Consider using `backoff_for_attempt(cursor.attempt - 1)` or starting the base delay at attempt 2 so the first retry is immediate and backoff kicks in only on repeated failures.

Preserve invoke-time backend availability classifications in `src/contexts/agent_execution/service.rs:383-408`. `map_invoke_error` currently converts every non-`Invocation*` error into `InvocationFailed { failure_class: TransportFailure }`, which discards the new `BackendUnavailable.failure_class` metadata. That means invoke-time `BackendUnavailable { failure_class: Some(BinaryNotFound) }` from `src/adapters/openrouter_backend.rs:396-401` or `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:789-800` still burns the retry budget instead of failing terminally.

Fix the process-backed OpenRouter command builder in `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:802-808` so a missing or blank `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` is treated as the same terminal infrastructure failure used elsewhere. This path currently only checks whether `std::env::var` returned `Ok`, so `""` is accepted and a missing key becomes `InvocationFailed { failure_class: TransportFailure }`. That contradicts `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1200-1208` and `src/adapters/openrouter_backend.rs:68-77`, and the stale expectation in `tests/unit/process_backend_test.rs:1394-1421` should be updated with it.

In engine.rs:4620, `backoff_for_attempt(cursor.attempt - 1)` causes the first retry (after the first failure, cursor.attempt=1) to get `backoff_for_attempt(0)` which returns `Duration::ZERO`. This means the first retry happens immediately with no delay, defeating the acceptance criterion that 'retry attempts have backoff delay between them.' The `-1` adjustment was introduced in round 10 (changed from `cursor.attempt`). Revert to `backoff_for_attempt(cursor.attempt)` so the first retry gets a 5s delay, or document the intentional zero-delay-first-retry design choice and update the acceptance criteria accordingly.

The two OpenRouter API key tests (`invoke_openrouter_without_api_key_returns_terminal_failure` and `invoke_openrouter_with_blank_api_key_returns_terminal_failure` in tests/unit/process_backend_test.rs) use `unsafe { std::env::set_var() }` and `unsafe { std::env::remove_var() }` without synchronization against other tests that may read OPENROUTER_API_KEY. While each test uses `#[tokio::test(flavor = "current_thread")]`, cargo runs test functions in parallel across threads. The existing `_guard`/restore pattern is a best-effort approach but can race with other tests. Consider using a shared mutex (like the existing `lock_path_mutex()` pattern used for PATH tests) to serialize all tests that modify OPENROUTER_API_KEY.

`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:4620` calls `retry_policy.backoff_for_attempt(cursor.attempt - 1)`, but `RetryPolicy::backoff_for_attempt()` is explicitly 1-indexed in `src/contexts/workflow_composition/retry_policy.rs:102-112`. On the first retry `cursor.attempt` is 1, so this computes attempt 0 and returns `Duration::ZERO`, making the first retry immediate and shifting every later delay down one step. That defeats the new backoff policy exactly where rate-limit recovery needs it most.

The tmux timeout path can report success even when cleanup failed. In `src/adapters/tmux.rs:534-541` the timeout branch ignores the result of `self.cancel(&request.invocation_id).await`. But `cancel()` removes the session from tracking up front at `src/adapters/tmux.rs:621-623` and can still fail later during SIGTERM/SIGKILL/`kill-session` cleanup at `src/adapters/tmux.rs:626-675`. If any of those steps fail, the code still returns a timeout error and drops the only tracking handle, so the hung tmux session/backend can remain alive after the caller has been told it was cleaned up.

The process-backed OpenRouter path still treats whitespace-only API keys as valid. `ProcessBackendAdapter::build_command()` only rejects `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` when `!k.is_empty()` fails at `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:802-805`, and `check_availability()` uses the same non-trimming check at `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1203-1206`. A value like `' '` therefore bypasses the new terminal `BinaryNotFound` handling and gets retried later as a transport failure. This is user-visible on the default production path because `build_backend_adapter()` still defaults to the `process` adapter in `src/composition/agent_execution_builder.rs:53-74` and returns `ProcessBackendAdapter` for that selector at `src/composition/agent_execution_builder.rs:110-113`. The direct HTTP adapter already uses `trim().is_empty()` at `src/adapters/openrouter_backend.rs:68-76`, so the two OpenRouter paths now disagree.

Add jitter to the exponential backoff in `RetryPolicy::backoff_for_attempt()` (retry_policy.rs:106-113). Currently the backoff is deterministic (5s, 10s, 20s, 40s, 60s). When multiple concurrent runs or panel members hit the same rate limit, they all retry at identical intervals, creating thundering-herd amplification on the already-stressed backend. Adding ±20-30% random jitter (e.g. `delay * uniform(0.7, 1.3)`) would stagger retries and improve recovery behavior under load.

In `tmux.rs` timeout handler (line 540), if `self.cancel()` returns Err, the code logs and continues to return the Timeout error — but `cancel()` calls `take_active_session()` first (removing the session from the tracking HashMap), then may fail during SIGTERM/SIGKILL. At that point the tmux session process is leaked (not killed, not tracked). Consider a fallback: if cancel fails, directly call `self.kill_session(&session.session_name).await` and `session.cleanup()` on the local Arc, since the session is still accessible in scope.

src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1048-1063 reports a timeout and removes the child from `active_children` before teardown is confirmed, and src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1477-1480 drops both `force_kill()` and `wait()` errors in the background reaper. If SIGKILL/`Child::kill()` fails, the hung backend keeps running but the adapter has already forgotten the handle, so the retry loop can start a second invocation against the same workspace while the first process is still alive. Timeout handling needs to synchronously confirm teardown, or at least surface teardown failure instead of treating the timeout as cleanly handled.

src/adapters/tmux.rs:534-562 swallows `self.cancel(...)` failures on timeout and still returns `FailureClass::Timeout`, but `cancel()` removes the session from `active_sessions` immediately at src/adapters/tmux.rs:633-636 before attempting SIGTERM/SIGKILL/`kill-session`. Any later cleanup error leaves an orphaned tmux session and stale runtime state that future retries can no longer cancel. Timeout cleanup failures should abort the attempt as a transport/teardown failure, or the session should stay tracked until teardown actually succeeds.

src/adapters/process_backend.rs:996-1005 classifies every `Command::spawn()` `ErrorKind::NotFound` as `FailureClass::BinaryNotFound`. `spawn()` also returns ENOENT when `request.working_dir` is missing, and for other startup-path failures such as a missing interpreter for an executable script. Those cases are not verified backend-binary misses, but they now become terminal `binary_not_found` failures and skip the expanded retry budget. Restrict `BinaryNotFound` to cases that actually prove the backend executable is missing; leave other spawn failures retryable or classify them separately.

Add test coverage for timeout enforcement and jitter as required by acceptance criteria. Specifically: (a) a test for `confirm_teardown` verifying that a killed process returns `teardown_confirmed=true` and a process that resists kill returns false; (b) a test for the unclean-timeout → TransportFailure mapping in the process backend timeout path; (c) a test verifying that jitter-enabled backoff produces values within the documented ±25% range (i.e., `backoff_for_attempt(1)` on the default policy returns a Duration in `[3.75s, 6.25s)`). The diff adds zero new test functions despite the acceptance criteria explicitly stating 'new tests cover timeout enforcement and retry backoff'.

In process_backend.rs ~line 1003, the `!binary.exists()` guard on the spawn ENOENT handler is unreliable when `binary` is a bare name (e.g., `PathBuf("claude")`) because `Path::exists()` resolves relative to CWD, not PATH. When no explicit search paths are configured, `resolve_binary` returns the bare name for OS PATH lookup. If spawn returns ENOENT because the working directory or script interpreter is missing (not the binary itself), and `./claude` also doesn't exist in CWD, the error is misclassified as `BinaryNotFound` (terminal) instead of `TransportFailure` (retryable). Fix: use `which::which()` or replicate PATH resolution before the `exists()` check, or guard with `self.search_paths.is_some()` so the check only applies when the binary path is known to be absolute.

src/contexts/workflow_composition/retry_policy.rs:122-142 applies jitter after `delay.min(self.backoff_cap)`. That lets `backoff_for_attempt()` exceed the configured cap: with the default settings, attempt 5 is capped to 60s and then multiplied by up to 1.25, so the engine can sleep for roughly 75s even though `DEFAULT_BACKOFF_CAP_SECS` at lines 32-33 is documented as the maximum. Re-cap after jitter, or apply jitter before the final `min()`.

src/adapters/process_backend.rs:526-553 and 997-1008 misclassify some real missing-binary failures. In ambient-PATH mode `resolve_binary()` returns a bare name like `claude`, but the new spawn error path uses `!binary.exists()` to decide whether ENOENT should be `BinaryNotFound`. `Path::exists()` on a bare name checks the current working directory, not PATH, so an unrelated `./claude` or `./codex` file in the repo can turn a true PATH miss into `TransportFailure` and consume the full retry budget instead of failing terminally.

details.message: backend invocation failed for contract 'plan_and_implement' via 'claude': schema validation failed for stage 'plan_and_implement': missing field `change_summary`

details.body: In `src/contexts/agent_execution/service.rs:210`, `request.timeout + Duration::from_secs(30)` uses `Duration::add` which panics on overflow. Replace with `request.timeout.saturating_add(Duration::from_secs(30))` to prevent a panic if a timeout value near `Duration::MAX` is ever passed. While practically unlikely with real timeout values, this is a one-character fix that eliminates a theoretical panic path in production code.

`src/adapters/process_backend.rs:412-418`, `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1058-1067`, and `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1513-1518` only kill the immediate child PID when a timeout fires. That does not kill the child's subprocess tree, so a wrapper shell or CLI that forks can leave grandchildren running after the invocation returns `Timeout`. This means the hard-timeout path still leaks backend processes/resources and does not fully satisfy the requirement that hanging backend invocations be killed. Start the backend in its own process group/job and terminate the whole group on timeout/cancel.

`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:4472-4481` persists the run as `Running`, then `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:4563-4641` records `stage_failed` and sleeps for backoff without any further snapshot update. If the worker is killed or the host restarts during that new 5-60s backoff window, the project is stranded in a `Running` snapshot even though no process is active, and `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:1147-1169` makes `run resume` refuse to recover it. Persist a resumable state before sleeping, or allow resume to recover stale `Running` snapshots after a retryable failure.

Contract: final_review:reviewer
Role: Reviewer

# Final Review Proposals

You are a code reviewer. Review the changes in this project for correctness, safety, and robustness.

Run `git diff` to see all changes, then read the key implementation files end-to-end. For each issue found, cite specific files and line numbers.

Ignore style and cosmetics — only report real bugs, safety problems, correctness gaps, and significant maintainability risks.

## Project Prompt

◐ ralph-burning-gn8 · Harden retry policy to prevent run exit on transient backend failures   [● P0 · IN_PROGRESS]
Owner: master · Type: task
Created: 2026-03-30 · Updated: 2026-03-30

## Goal

Prevent ralph-burning runs from exiting/failing on transient backend issues (Claude/Codex CLI flakiness, rate limits, timeouts) by increasing retry limits and adding missing timeout enforcement.

## Investigation Findings

The system does NOT crash — it exits gracefully after exhausting retry attempts. The problem is the retry budget is too small for real-world backend flakiness:

### Current retry limits (`src/contexts/workflow_composition/retry_policy.rs:34-47`)

| FailureClass | Max Attempts | Retryable |
|---|---|---|
| TransportFailure | 3 | yes |
| SchemaValidationFailure | 2 | yes |
| DomainValidationFailure | 2 | yes |
| Timeout | 2 | yes |
| Cancellation | 1 | no |
| QaReviewOutcomeFailure | 1 | no |

A single transient Claude CLI failure (invalid JSON, exit code 1) gets only 2-3 tries before the entire run is marked as `Failed` and stops. In long-running tasks with 20+ rounds, this is too fragile.

### No hard timeout on backend invocations

**Critical gap:** `src/adapters/process_backend.rs` spawns backend processes and waits indefinitely (`active_child.wait()` at ~line 975). The timeout value in the invocation request is recorded in metadata but never enforced. A hanging Claude/Codex process blocks the entire run forever.

### All non-zero exit codes are TransportFailure

`src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1138-1196` classifies ANY non-zero exit (including exit 127 = binary not found, signal 11 = segfault) as `TransportFailure`. No granularity — a missing binary gets the same 3 retries as a transient network error.

## Changes Required

### 1. Increase retry limits for transient failures
**File:** `src/contexts/workflow_composition/retry_policy.rs:34-47`

- `TransportFailure`: 3 → 5 (network issues, rate limits)
- `SchemaValidationFailure`: 2 → 3 (invalid JSON from CLI)
- `Timeout`: 2 → 3 (backend slowness)

### 2. Add hard timeout enforcement on backend invocations
**File:** `src/adapters/process_backend.rs` (~line 975)

Wrap `spawn_and_wait()` / `active_child.wait()` in `tokio::time::timeout()` using the request's timeout value. If the timeout fires, kill the child process and return `FailureClass::Timeout`.

### 3. Differentiate fatal vs transient exit codes
**File:** `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1138-1196`

Add classification:
- Exit 127 (binary not found) → new `FailureClass` or mark as non-retryable TransportFailure
- Signal-killed (SIGSEGV, SIGABRT) → TransportFailure (retryable, likely OOM or transient)
- Exit 1 with known error patterns (rate limit, auth) → TransportFailure (retryable)

### 4. Add exponential backoff between retries
**File:** `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs` (retry loop ~line 4620)

Currently retries are immediate. Add a short delay (e.g. 5s, 15s, 30s) between retry attempts to avoid hammering a rate-limited backend.

## Acceptance Criteria

- TransportFailure allows 5 attempts before run failure
- SchemaValidationFailure allows 3 attempts before run failure
- Backend invocations have a hard timeout (configurable, default ~10min)
- Hanging backend processes are killed after timeout
- Exit 127 (binary not found) fails immediately without retrying
- Retry attempts have backoff delay between them
- Existing tests pass; new tests cover timeout enforcement and retry backoff

## Authoritative JSON Schema

The JSON schema below is authoritative. Return only JSON that conforms exactly to it.

```json
{
  "$schema": "http://json-schema.org/draft-07/schema#",
  "definitions": {
    "FinalReviewProposal": {
      "description": "A raw amendment proposal returned by a final-review reviewer.",
      "properties": {
        "body": {
          "description": "The free-form amendment body. This is canonicalized and deduplicated by the orchestration layer before voting.",
          "type": "string"
        },
        "rationale": {
          "description": "Optional reviewer-provided rationale for why this amendment matters.",
          "type": [
            "string",
            "null"
          ]
        }
      },
      "required": [
        "body"
      ],
      "type": "object"
    }
  },
  "description": "Payload returned by each final-review reviewer during proposal collection.",
  "properties": {
    "amendments": {
      "description": "Proposed amendments. An empty list means \"no amendments\".",
      "items": {
        "$ref": "#/definitions/FinalReviewProposal"
      },
      "type": "array"
    },
    "summary": {
      "description": "Summary of the reviewer pass.",
      "type": "string"
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "amendments",
    "summary"
  ],
  "title": "FinalReviewProposalPayload",
  "type": "object"
}
```

Return ONLY valid JSON matching the following schema:

details.body: BUG: In `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:4634-4636`, the pre-backoff snapshot code sets `snapshot.active_run = None` before sleeping. After the sleep, the loop continues back to line 4473, which calls `carry_forward_active_run(snapshot, ...)`. This function calls `current_active_run(snapshot)` (line 590), which requires `snapshot.active_run.is_some()` and returns `Err(AppError::CorruptRecord)` when it's `None`. Every retry with non-zero backoff will immediately fail with a CorruptRecord error, making the backoff feature worse than useless — it converts a retryable failure into a fatal one. Fix: after the sleep (and before `continue`), restore `snapshot.active_run` from `snapshot.interrupted_run` (which was saved by `preserve_interrupted_run` at line 4634) and reset `snapshot.status = RunStatus::Running`.

Missing test coverage for the backoff snapshot round-trip. The new backoff snapshot code at `engine.rs:4622-4664` is a critical path (it's the only code path that sets `active_run = None` mid-retry-loop) but has no integration or unit test. Add a test that exercises a stage failure with backoff enabled, verifying that: (1) the snapshot written during backoff has `status = Failed` and `active_run = None`, (2) after the backoff sleep, the retry loop successfully restores state and re-enters the stage, and (3) if the process is killed during backoff, `run resume` can recover from the persisted snapshot.

Update bead `ralph-burning-wt3` in `.beads/issues.jsonl:100` to explicitly define how final-review quorum changes when a reviewer backend becomes unavailable at runtime. The current code only skips failing reviewers when `member.required == false` (`src/contexts/workflow_composition/final_review.rs:225-235`, `src/contexts/workflow_composition/final_review.rs:427-437`) and still fails if executed reviewers/voters drop below `min_reviewers` (`src/contexts/workflow_composition/final_review.rs:281-286`, `src/contexts/workflow_composition/final_review.rs:473-478`). With the defaults, `DEFAULT_MIN_REVIEWERS` is 2 and both final-review backends are required (`src/contexts/workspace_governance/config.rs:25`, `src/contexts/workspace_governance/config.rs:1473-1477`), so the acceptance criterion to proceed with only one reviewer is impossible unless the bead also changes required-member handling and the effective minimum/consensus rules.

Expand the implementation scope described in `.beads/issues.jsonl:100` so “apply to all multi-backend stages” covers the actual runtime-failure paths outside final review. `completion` currently propagates the first invocation error from any completer and only checks `min_completers` after successful executions (`src/contexts/workflow_composition/completion.rs:109-182`), and `prompt_review` does the same for validator failures (`src/contexts/workflow_composition/prompt_review.rs:165-237`). If the new failure class is only handled in `src/contexts/workflow_composition/final_review.rs`, the same persistent backend outage will still abort other multi-backend panels.

Tighten the backend-unavailable detection requirements in `.beads/issues.jsonl:100` so classification only inspects backend-generated error channels on failed invocations, not arbitrary stdout text. `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1478-1487` already treats stdout as an error only when the backend sets `is_error == true`; otherwise stdout can contain ordinary model output. Matching broad substrings like `billing` or `credits` across raw stdout/stderr would create false positives for normal task content.

The `classify_exit_failure` function in `process_backend.rs:1496-1501` only special-cases exit code 127 (BinaryNotFound). All other non-zero exits AND signal-killed processes (where `status.code()` returns `None`) are classified as `TransportFailure` (retryable, 5 attempts). While the spec explicitly says signal kills should be retryable, consider logging a warning when a process is killed by a signal (SIGSEGV=11, SIGABRT=6) since repeated signal kills strongly suggest a deterministic crash rather than a transient issue. A warning would help operators notice this pattern without changing retry behavior.

Treat failure to persist the pre-backoff snapshot as fatal in `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:4622-4644` instead of ignoring `write_run_snapshot()`. The new crash-resume path only works if that `Failed`/`interrupted_run` snapshot actually reaches disk; today the write error is silently swallowed, so a kill or crash during backoff can still leave the project stranded in the old `Running` snapshot with no resumable state.

Preserve retry-attempt state across a crash during backoff. `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:4622-4644` now writes resumable mid-retry snapshots, but `derive_resume_state()` rebuilds every failed resume with `attempt: 1` in `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:5851-5857`. A restart during the backoff window therefore replenishes the retry budget and can exceed the configured max-attempt limit for the stage.

details.message: backend invocation failed for contract 'plan_and_implement' via 'claude': schema validation failed for stage 'plan_and_implement': missing field `change_summary`

details.body: In engine.rs ~line 4643, the snapshot write during retry backoff was changed from `let _ = write_run_snapshot(...)` to `write_run_snapshot(...)?`. This is a resilience regression: the snapshot write here is a best-effort operation to allow `run resume` to recover if the process crashes during the backoff sleep. If the snapshot write fails transiently (e.g., momentary disk pressure), the `?` propagates the error and aborts the entire retry loop — exactly the fragility this project is trying to fix. Other snapshot write failures in the same function are wrapped in `fail_run_result(...)` which does proper journal accounting, but this bare `?` bypasses that. Recommendation: either revert to `let _ =` (best-effort) with a `tracing::warn!` on failure, or wrap the error with `fail_run_result` for proper cleanup if the write is now considered mandatory.

The `classify_exit_failure` refactor and the `derive_resume_state` attempt counter preservation lack unit tests. `classify_exit_failure` is a public(crate) function with clearly testable behavior (exit 127 → BinaryNotFound, exit 1 → TransportFailure, signal-killed → TransportFailure with warning log) but has no test coverage. The `derive_resume_state` change to track `retryable_failed_attempts` should have a test that constructs a journal with StageFailed events (will_retry=true) and verifies the resulting cursor.attempt is correctly set to failed_attempt+1. The acceptance criteria explicitly require 'new tests cover timeout enforcement and retry backoff.'

High: `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:5794-5885` now reconstructs the next retry attempt from a map keyed only by `(stage_id, cycle)`, but the engine can legitimately revisit the same stage in the same cycle when a completion round advances (`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:5835-5843`), and `StageCursor::advance_completion_round` explicitly resets `attempt` to 1 (`src/shared/domain.rs:806-815`). Because `stage_failed` events also persist only `stage_id`, `cycle`, and `attempt` (`src/contexts/project_run_record/journal.rs:186-209`), a planning/final-review stage that needed retries in round 1 will be resumed in round 2 at `attempt = prior_failed_attempt + 1`, silently burning or exhausting the retry budget across logically separate executions. The retry-resume key needs to include `completion_round` (and/or be cleared on successful stage completion) before this is safe.

Medium: the new backoff snapshot write in `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:4621-4643` now uses `?` directly, so a `write_run_snapshot` failure aborts `execute_stage_with_retry` immediately. The last durable snapshot for that stage is still the earlier `Running` snapshot written at `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:4471-4481`, and the caller just propagates the error with `await?` (`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:3278-3307`). That means a transient snapshot-store I/O error during the retry backoff window can leave the project stranded in a non-resumable `Running` state, which is exactly the condition this new backoff snapshot is supposed to prevent. This path needs the same `StageCommitFailed -> fail_run_result(...)` handling as the stage-entered snapshot write.

Medium: `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:999-1009` classifies any bare-command `spawn()` `ErrorKind::NotFound` as `BinaryNotFound`. The comment on those lines already notes that `spawn()` returns `ENOENT` for more than just a missing executable, including a missing working directory or missing script interpreter. If the project/worktree directory disappears after availability preflight, the backend failure will be reported as terminal `BinaryNotFound` and will not be retried, even though the binary itself may still be present. This needs an extra cwd/interpreter check or a more conservative fallback to `TransportFailure` unless the missing-binary case is positively proven.

In `derive_resume_state` (engine.rs ~line 5843), the `CycleAdvanced` handler does not clear stale entries from `retryable_failed_attempts` for prior cycles. While functionally correct (the HashMap key includes cycle number, so old entries won't match new-cycle lookups), the entries accumulate across cycles and are never reclaimed. In runs with many remediation cycles, this is a bounded but unnecessary memory growth. Add `retryable_failed_attempts.retain(|&(_, c), _| c >= current_cycle);` after updating `current_cycle` in the CycleAdvanced handler, matching the pattern used by CompletionRoundAdvanced's `.clear()`.

The `apply_jitter` function (retry_policy.rs:134-143) uses `SystemTime::now().subsec_nanos() % 1000` as a randomness source. On systems where the clock resolution is coarser than 1μs (e.g., some VMs or containers), multiple calls within the same millisecond will produce identical jitter values, defeating the thundering-herd mitigation the jitter is designed to provide. Consider using `thread_rng()` from the `rand` crate (already a transitive dependency) or at minimum `Instant::now()` which is monotonic, or mix in the attempt number to ensure distinct values across consecutive retries.

Clear stale retry-attempt bookkeeping on terminal stage failures in src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:5825-5841 and src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:5897-5902. The new `retryable_failed_attempts` map records `will_retry=true` failures, but it is never cleared when the same stage later emits `will_retry=false` after exhausting retries or hitting a terminal failure. That makes `run resume` restart at the old retry count instead of attempt 1. Repro: `cargo run --quiet --features test-stub -- conformance run --filter SC-RESUME-005` now fails with `expected first resumed stage attempt=1, got 5` (see the scenario expectation in src/contexts/conformance_spec/scenarios.rs:4539-4642).

Do not treat the pre-backoff snapshot write as best-effort in src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:4622-4656. By that point the `stage_failed` event has already been durably appended at src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:4563-4600, so if `write_run_snapshot` fails and the process dies during the backoff sleep, the journal says the run failed/retrying while `run.json` stays in its old state. That reintroduces the exact 'non-resumable after crash during backoff' condition this snapshot is supposed to prevent.

details.message: backend invocation failed for contract 'plan_and_implement' via 'claude': schema validation failed for stage 'plan_and_implement': missing field `change_summary`

details.body: Add a test for the snapshot-write-failure-during-backoff path in the engine retry loop. The change at engine.rs:4649 converted `write_run_snapshot` from best-effort (log+swallow) to mandatory (`?` propagation), which is a behavioral change: a transient disk error during backoff now aborts the entire run instead of continuing. The project already has a `FailingSnapshotWrite` test fixture (conformance_spec/scenarios.rs:17922). A targeted test should inject a snapshot write failure during a retry-backoff window and verify that: (a) the engine returns an error rather than silently continuing, and (b) a subsequent `derive_resume_state` from the journal alone correctly reconstructs the retry position, confirming the journal is authoritative even when the snapshot is stale.

src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:4643-4649 and src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:3278-3307: the new `run_snapshot_write.write_run_snapshot(...)?` in the retry-backoff path now bubbles snapshot-write failures straight out of `execute_stage_with_retry`. Because the caller also awaits that function with `?`, these errors bypass `fail_run_result`, so the already-appended `stage_failed` event is left on disk while `run.json` stays in its previous `running` state. A disk-full/permission/fs hiccup here now strands the run in the exact unresumable state this change was trying to prevent. Route this through `fail_run_result` (or otherwise guarantee a resumable failed snapshot) instead of propagating the raw write error.

src/contexts/workflow_composition/retry_policy.rs:134-155: the new jitter implementation does not actually fix coarse-clock correlation. It still derives the delay entirely from `SystemTime::now().subsec_nanos()`, and the hash-mixing step is deterministic, so calls that happen within the same clock tick still get identical jitter values. On 1ms/10ms-resolution platforms, concurrent retries will remain synchronized despite the new comments claiming otherwise. Use a real entropy source or mix in a monotonic counter/thread-local RNG if same-tick calls need to diverge.

In the snapshot write error recovery path (engine.rs ~line 4657-4683), if `fail_run`'s own snapshot write at line 5133 also fails (e.g. persistent disk I/O error), the `?` propagates the error without emitting a `run_failed` journal event and without any snapshot reaching disk — potentially leaving the project in an inconsistent state where the journal has a `stage_failed` event but the on-disk snapshot is stale. Consider wrapping `fail_run`'s snapshot write in a best-effort fallback (e.g. log the double-failure and still emit the journal event), or at minimum add a comment documenting this known limitation and why it's acceptable.

details.message: backend invocation failed for contract 'plan_and_implement' via 'claude': Claude returned empty result with no structured_output (contract: plan_and_implement, stdout_len: 907, session_policy: ReuseIfAllowed)

details.body: The `classify_exit_tests` module in `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:2954-3005` uses `std::os::unix::process::ExitStatusExt` unconditionally (no `#[cfg(unix)]` gate on the module). This will fail to compile on non-Unix platforms. Add `#[cfg(unix)]` to the `classify_exit_tests` module to match the conditional compilation pattern used elsewhere in the file (e.g. the `#[cfg(unix)]` block inside `classify_exit_failure` itself).

`src/adapters/process_backend.rs:985-992`, `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1062-1088`, `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:412-419`, and `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1530-1536` still only kill/reap the direct child PID. The command is spawned without its own process group/job object, and the timeout path only calls `send_sigkill()`/`Child::kill()` on that one child before declaring teardown confirmed. If the backend shells out or backgrounds a helper that inherits stdout/stderr, the parent can exit while the descendant keeps running; the invocation is reported as timed out/retryable, but the leaked descendant survives into the next retry. That misses the acceptance criterion that hanging backend processes are killed after timeout.

`src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1064-1088` drops the invocation from `active_children` and returns immediately when timeout teardown is not confirmed, but unlike `cancel()` there is no background reap fallback. The existing cancel path explicitly keeps reaping in the background at `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1380-1390` via `spawn_background_reap()` (`src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1520-1525`); the timeout path does not. A slow-to-die child can therefore outlive tracking and subsequent retries/cancellations will no longer be able to find it.

Timeout-like failures that come back as `TransportFailure` lose their diagnostics. The timeout branch intentionally downgrades unconfirmed teardown to `TransportFailure` at `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1068-1075`, but `invoke()` only preserves failed artifacts when `error.failure_class() == Timeout` and otherwise calls `prepared.cleanup()` at `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1259-1266` (same pattern in the stale-session retry path at `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1298-1308`). That deletes the schema/last-message temp files for the hardest timeout failures to debug.
